content.text: The CODEOWNERS file is a GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket feature that maps file paths to required reviewers, automatically requesting reviews from the right people when a pull request modifies specific files. In agentic AI repositories, where prompt templates and tool implementations are first-class artifacts that require domain-specific review expertise, a well-structured CODEOWNERS configuration becomes a critical safety mechanism that prevents behavioral changes from shipping without appropriate ML, safety, or platform review.

) that maps glob patterns to GitHub teams or individual usernames. When a pull request modifies files matching a pattern, GitHub automatically requests reviews from the mapped owners and marks their approval as a required check. This review requirement is enforced by GitHub's branch protection rules, blocking merge until all code owners whose files were touched have approved.

For agentic AI repositories, the CODEOWNERS file is extended beyond the standard application code ownership to cover four distinct artifact categories: the prompt registry (YAML or JSON files defining system messages and user instructions), the tool implementations (Python or TypeScript files that agents call), the evaluation catalogue (test datasets and grading scripts), and the model configuration (files specifying which model version each agent uses). Each category has different review expertise requirements, and CODEOWNERS enforces them automatically.

Without CODEOWNERS, a developer can modify a system prompt to fix a formatting issue and inadvertently remove a safety guardrail that was embedded in the prompt's instructions. The change ships because it looks like a minor text edit in the PR diff and no ML engineer happened to review it. CODEOWNERS prevents this by making ML team review mandatory for any PR that touches the
directory, regardless of how small the change appears.

The same logic applies to tool implementations. A platform engineer adding retry logic to a browser tool might accidentally change the tool's output schema, breaking the agent's parsing assumptions. With CODEOWNERS, any PR touching
requires platform team review and any PR touching
(which parses tool outputs) also requires platform team review, catching the schema mismatch before it reaches main.
Automatic review routing: GitHub requests the right reviewers without any manual @mention in the PR description.
Enforcement at merge time: CODEOWNERS approvals are required checks; a PR cannot merge until all affected owners have approved.
Domain expertise alignment: prompt engineers review prompt changes, platform engineers review tool changes, safety team reviews eval changes.
Visible ownership: the CODEOWNERS file is the single source of truth for who is responsible for each directory.
Audit trail: GitHub's review history shows which code owner approved each change to a prompt or tool.
Reduced cognitive load: developers do not need to know who owns what; they open a PR and GitHub tells them.
Review bottleneck risk: if a CODEOWNERS team is small or unavailable, PRs that touch their files queue up and block shipping.
Overly broad patterns: a pattern like '*.yaml' that captures both infrastructure and prompt files creates unwanted review requirements.
Team maintenance: as teams reorganise, CODEOWNERS entries for disbanded or renamed teams silently stop working.
Shared file conflicts: a file that legitimately belongs to two teams creates ambiguity about which team's approval is sufficient.
No partial ownership: CODEOWNERS ownership applies to whole files; it cannot require ML review for only the 'system_prompt' field in a configuration file that also contains infrastructure settings.

When a PR is opened, GitHub reads the CODEOWNERS file, checks which files in the diff match each pattern, and requests reviews from the corresponding owners. The patterns are evaluated from bottom to top, with the last matching pattern taking precedence. Approval from a team member counts as the team's approval. Branch protection rules with 'Require review from Code Owners' enabled block the merge button until all required owners have approved.

For agentic AI repositories, the CODEOWNERS file is structured in layers. A catch-all pattern at the top assigns broad ownership to the platform team. Specific patterns below override it for AI-specific directories, assigning ML team ownership to prompts and safety team co-ownership to evaluations. The most specific patterns at the bottom handle edge cases like individual critical files (the main system prompt, the production model config) that require named-individual approval.

attrs.alt: CODEOWNERS directory ownership map showing review routing for prompt registries, tools, and eval suites

content.text: The ownership model has four zones: the prompt registry (ML team ownership), tool implementations (platform team ownership), evaluation catalogue (ML + safety team co-ownership), and application source code (platform team ownership with exceptions). Co-ownership means all named teams must approve, providing a double-review gate on safety-critical artifacts. The PR routing logic is automatic: GitHub inspects changed files, resolves ownership zones, and requests the appropriate reviews.
Any repository where prompts, tools, or evaluations are stored as files alongside application code.
Teams with distinct domain expertise boundaries (ML engineers own prompts; platform engineers own tools).
Repositories where behavioral changes must receive safety team sign-off before merging.
Organisations with compliance requirements that mandate documented review by specific roles for AI artifact changes.
Teams large enough that developers do not inherently know who to ask for review on any given file.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Evaluated bottom-to-top; last matching pattern wins.

# Default: platform team owns all code
*                                    @org/platform-team

# Source code: platform owns routing and agent logic
src/agent/                           @org/platform-team
src/routing/                         @org/platform-team
src/infra/                           @org/platform-team

# Prompt registry: ML team + prompt reviewers required
prompts/                             @org/ml-team @org/prompt-reviewers
prompts/system/                      @org/ml-team @org/prompt-reviewers @org/safety-team

# Tool implementations: platform team
tools/                               @org/platform-team
tools/api-client/                    @org/platform-team @org/security-team

# Evaluation catalogue: ML team + safety team
eval/                                @org/ml-team
eval/safety/                         @org/ml-team @org/safety-team
eval/red-team/                       @org/safety-team

# Model configuration: explicit named reviewers
config/models.yaml                   @alice @bob @org/ml-team

# CI/CD: DevOps ownership
.github/workflows/                   @org/devops-team
.github/CODEOWNERS                   @org/devops-team @org/platform-team

# .github/branch-protection.yml (via Terraform or gh CLI)
# Branch protection ensuring CODEOWNERS is respected

# Use gh CLI to configure:
# gh api repos/org/repo/branches/main/protection --method PUT -f ...

required_pull_request_reviews:
  required_approving_review_count: 1
  require_code_owner_reviews: true     # KEY: enables CODEOWNERS enforcement
  dismiss_stale_reviews: true          # re-request review after new push
  require_last_push_approval: true

required_status_checks:
  strict: true
  contexts:
    - "lint / commitlint"
    - "test / unit"
    - "eval / regression"

restrictions: null  # all org members can push to non-protected branches
enforce_admins: true
Scenario: a developer opens a PR that modifies three files:
GitHub evaluates the CODEOWNERS file and requests reviews from:
@org/ml-team and @org/prompt-reviewers and @org/safety-team for the financial system prompt (matches 'prompts/system/')
@org/platform-team and @org/security-team for the banking API tool (matches 'tools/api-client/')

The PR cannot merge until at least one member of each of those teams has approved. If the safety team has not reviewed the system prompt change, the merge button remains blocked regardless of any other approvals.

A legal AI company building a contract analysis agent discovered after a production incident that a developer had modified the system prompt to improve formatting and accidentally removed the clause that instructed the model not to provide legal advice. The change looked innocuous in the diff and was approved by a platform engineer who was not aware of the safety significance of the removed text.
After the incident, the company added a CODEOWNERS entry requiring both
approval for any change to the

directory. They also added a required reviewers entry for two named senior ML engineers for any change to the main system prompt file. The protection was implemented in 30 minutes and has prevented three near-misses in the subsequent year where developers modified the system prompt without understanding its safety implications.
Using 'CODEOWNERS' in the repo root without branch protection:
the file alone does nothing; branch protection with 'Require review from Code Owners' must be enabled.
Assigning individual usernames instead of teams:
individual usernames become single points of failure when the person is on vacation. Always use GitHub team references (@org/team-name).
a bare '*' that assigns everything to one team creates review fatigue and causes owners to rubber-stamp PRs rather than reviewing them carefully.
Not updating CODEOWNERS when directories are renamed or reorganised:
GitHub silently ignores CODEOWNERS patterns for paths that do not exist, leaving areas unowned.
files like the main system prompt should require multiple team approvals, not just one team's approval.
Define the ownership model before creating the CODEOWNERS file; document which teams own which artifact categories in the team wiki.
Use GitHub teams (not individuals) for all ownership entries to ensure continuous coverage.
Apply the principle of least privilege: assign the narrowest ownership zone that still ensures domain expert review.
Add a CI job that validates the CODEOWNERS file syntax using github-codeowners-validator on every PR.
Review and update the CODEOWNERS file whenever the directory structure changes; add a 'chore(codeowners):' commit type for these updates.
Test CODEOWNERS routing by opening a draft PR that touches each key directory and verifying that the expected teams are requested.
Document the ownership model in the project README so new team members understand who to consult about each type of change.

CODEOWNERS evaluation is performed by GitHub's infrastructure on every PR open and update event. It adds no latency to local development workflows. For very large CODEOWNERS files (thousands of patterns), GitHub processes them synchronously during the PR webhook handling; this is rarely a bottleneck in practice. At monorepo scale, use GitHub's CODEOWNERS path pattern specificity to ensure ownership resolution is unambiguous—avoid patterns that could match the same file in multiple ways.

CODEOWNERS is a security control when paired with branch protection. It prevents unauthorized modification of prompt templates and tool implementations by requiring domain-expert approval. For maximum security, enable 'Restrict pushes that create matching branches' and 'Require deployments to succeed before merging' in addition to CODEOWNERS approval requirements.

Reliability requires keeping the CODEOWNERS file accurate. An outdated entry that points to a disbanded team results in PRs being automatically assigned to non-existent reviewers, which GitHub handles by requiring any org member to approve—effectively removing the protection. Automate a weekly CI check that validates all team references in the CODEOWNERS file against the current GitHub organisation team list.

Static CODEOWNERS files require manual updates when teams change. A complementary approach uses a GitHub Actions workflow that dynamically validates whether the right reviews occurred before allowing merge, going beyond static file-based ownership:
Audit your CODEOWNERS coverage periodically to identify unowned files—especially newly created prompt or tool files that were not added to the CODEOWNERS mapping:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/audit-codeowners.sh - Find files with no CODEOWNERS match
# Requires: npm install -g codeowners-validator

echo "=== CODEOWNERS Coverage Audit ==="
echo ""

# Validate CODEOWNERS syntax
npx codeowners-validator .github/CODEOWNERS
echo ""

# Find prompt files with no CODEOWNERS match
echo "Checking prompt files for CODEOWNERS coverage..."
find prompts/ -name "*.yaml" -o -name "*.json" | while read f; do
  owner=$(npx github-codeowners owner "$f" 2>/dev/null)
  if [ -z "$owner" ] || [ "$owner" = "none" ]; then
    echo "  UNOWNED: $f"
  fi
done

# Find tool files with no CODEOWNERS match
echo ""
echo "Checking tool files for CODEOWNERS coverage..."
find tools/ -name "*.py" -o -name "*.ts" | while read f; do
  owner=$(npx github-codeowners owner "$f" 2>/dev/null)
  if [ -z "$owner" ] || [ "$owner" = "none" ]; then
    echo "  UNOWNED: $f"
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "Audit complete. Add unowned files to .github/CODEOWNERS."

Ownership in CODEOWNERS should align with on-call responsibilities. Teams that own prompts or tools in CODEOWNERS should also be on-call for incidents involving those components. Maintain a synchronisation script that validates this alignment:
Extract all CODEOWNERS team references and compare them to the PagerDuty on-call schedule.
Alert when a team is listed in CODEOWNERS for a safety-critical file but has no on-call rotation covering those hours.
Require that every CODEOWNERS team entry has a corresponding on-call escalation policy before it can be added.
Review CODEOWNERS alignment quarterly as part of the team's incident response preparation process.
CODEOWNERS maps file paths to required reviewers, automatically routing PR reviews to domain experts when AI artifacts change.
Store prompts, tools, and evaluations in dedicated directories to enable clean ownership zones in the CODEOWNERS file.
Co-ownership (multiple teams listed for one path) creates multi-party approval gates on safety-critical files like system prompts.
Branch protection with 'Require review from Code Owners' must be enabled for CODEOWNERS to have any enforcement effect.
Use GitHub teams rather than individual usernames to avoid single points of failure in the review chain.
Validate the CODEOWNERS file syntax in CI and audit team references weekly to keep ownership accurate as teams evolve.
